column1: Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017,15.0,2016,table,"Motivations, Sources of Information and Booking Processes",general_tourism,"Leisure Visitors
Almost one-third (30%) of leisure visitors were motivated to visit Lesotho by
Mountains/High Places. A further 22% were motivated by the Scenery. Culture was a
stronger draw for the European and North American markets than those living in Africa.
One quarter (25%) of all leisure visitors sourced information about their trip from Friends and
Relatives. Previous Visits to Lesotho, Websites (not www.visitlesotho.travel) and Guide
Books were used by a further 50% of visitors.
Tourists from Europe were more likely to obtain information from Guide Books than any
other source. Tourists from the USA were most likely to use websites, and were the
strongest users of TripAdvisor and other social media (YouTube, Facebook and Instagram).
Almost all trips were organised by the tourists themselves (93%). Only 10% of leisure
tourists from European markets and the USA used a travel agent or tour operator.",12.0,1.0,"Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017
column3: Motivations, Sources of Information and Booking Processes"

column1: Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017,17.0,2016,table,Length of Stay in Lesotho,visitor_profile,"All Overnight Visitors (Tourists)
The average length of stay of all tourists to Lesotho was 5.3 nights.
Tourists from the USA stayed the longest at 12.7 nights, followed by the Rest of Africa (8.4
nights) and Rest of Asia/Pacific (7.2 nights).
Of the key markets, tourists from the UK stayed the second longest at 6 nights, followed by
South Africa at 4.9 nights. Tourists from the Netherlands spent the shortest length of time in
Leisure tourists stayed an average of 3.5 nights, with Business and VFR tourists spending
an average of 8 nights in Lesotho.
Leisure tourists from the USA stayed
the longest in this group (6.5 nights)
Tourists from South Africa travelling for VFR stayed the longest, at 7.9 nights, followed by
religion at 6.5 nights. Leisure Tourists from South Africa only spent 3.5 nights in Lesotho.",16.0,1.0,Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017
Katse Dam was the most visited place by overnight visitors (tourists), with 15%. Afri Ski
Sani Pass and Semonkong were all visited by 10% or more.
Whilst Katse Dam was the most popular place for South Africa tourists, Thaba Bosiu was the
most popular place for tourists from Botswana, Malealea, was most popular with tourists
from Netherlands and Germany, and Semonkong was most popular for tourists from the UK
Leisure tourists typically went to Katse Dam, Afri Ski and Sani Pass, whilst VFR tourists
were most likely to visit Thaba Bosiu. Those visiting for Business were most likely to travel
to Mohale Dam, and Religion tourists typically went to Malealea and the Bushmen Paintings.
With the exception of tourists from South Africa, the most popular destination for most
Leisure tourists was Malealea. Afri Ski was most popular with South Africa tourists, and
Semonkong with tourists from the USA.
Almost 50% of all South Africa tourists travelled to Katse Dam, Afri-Ski or Sani Pass. These
were by far the most popular attractions. However, it should be noted that 1 in 5 (20%) VFR
tourists went to Thaba Bosiu, and almost 17% of Business tourists went to Mohale Dam.",18.0,1.0,Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017
General Sightseeing was the most popular activity of overnight visitors to Lesotho
accounting for 27% of all trips. A further 20% went Hiking, and 11% Pony Trekking.
General Sightseeing was most popular with tourists from Botswana (48%). Hiking was the
most popular activity with almost all the main European markets and the USA.
Amongst Leisure tourists, General Sightseeing accounted for 28% of all activities, followed
by Hiking with 25%. The top two activities for VFR tourists were General Sightseeing (26%)
and Wedding/Funeral (11%), although 50% said they did not participate in any activities.
When considering the main activity only for Leisure tourists, the top five most popular were
General Sightseeing, Hiking, Skiing, 4x4 Trekking and Biking. These are therefore the clear
Lesotho offers very different activities at different times of the year. Analysis by month
clearly shows the popularity of Skiing in July-August, Biking in December, fishing in January
April, and Rock Climbing in June-August.
Amongst the Leisure tourists, the top five activities undertaken were: General Sightseeing
Hiking, Pony Trekking, 4x4 Trekking, and Skiing. Biking came in at 6th place (6% of all
visitors). Only 3% of Leisure tourists said they did not participate in any activity.
Amongst South Africa Leisure tourists, the popularity of activities is broadly the same as for
all Leisure visitors, except Biking replaces Pony Trekking in the top five.",21.0,1.0,Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017
Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017,23.0,2016,table,Accommodation Types Used by Visitors to Lesotho,accommodation,"All Overnight Visitors (Tourists)
39% of all overnight visitors stayed in a Hotel/Lodge when visiting Lesotho. A further 33%
stay with Friends/Family, and 10% in Bed and Breakfast.
Tourists from South Africa were almost equally likely to stay in a Hotel/Lodge or with
Friends/Family, whilst those from Botswana were much more likely to stay with
Tourists from the European markets strongly preferred Hotel/Lodge as their accommodation.
Almost three-quarters (71%) of Leisure tourists stayed in a Hotel/Lodge, whilst exactly three
quarters (75%) of VFR tourists stayed with Friends/Family. Business tourists mainly
preferred a Hotel/Lodge (62%), although 16% stayed in Bed & Breakfast. Over two-thirds
(69%) of Religion tourists stayed in a Church/School.
Leisure tourists from all the main markets, with the exception of the USA, strongly preferred
Hotel/Lodge accommodation. Leisure tourists from the USA did use Hotel/Lodge
accommodation, but also widely used Bed & Breakfast and Camping accommodation, as

column1: Private Car/4x4 was the most popular method of transport around Lesotho, used by over
one-half (55%) of all tourists. Public transport was used by a further 24%.
Tourists from Botswana were most likely to use Private Car/4x4 (81%), whilst the main mode
of transport of all the main non-African markets was Hire Car/4x4. Tourists from
Netherlands and Germany were the most likely to rent their own vehicle.
Three-quarters (75%) of tourists travelling for Leisure used Private Car/4x4 to travel around
Lesotho, with 87% of South Africa Leisure tourists using this form of transport. Around two
thirds of European Leisure tourists used Hire Car/4x4, whilst only around one-half of those
from the USA did, with Public Transport being used by 13%.
The purpose of visit of South Africa tourists determined their choice of transport. Nearly all
(87%) of Leisure tourists used Private Car/4x4, whilst VFR (and Religion) tourists were
broadly split 50:50 between Private Car/4x4 and Public Transport. Business tourists mainly
used Private Car/4x4 (42%) and Company Car/4x4 (30%).",16.0,1.0,Visitor-Exit-Survey-2016-2017

Perception-Survey-Report-2024.pdf,,2024,report_text,Perception-Survey-Report-2024.pdf,arrivals,"12Foreword It is with great pleasure that we present this insightful report that is crafted to unveil tourists' perceptions in Lesotho. At its core, this report will reflect the government’s steadfast commitment to understand the diverse needs and preferences of tourists, harnessing its knowledge in order to promote sustainable tourism development. This report presents an analysis of motivations inspiring visitors to choose Lesotho as their destination of choice as well as their experiences and levels of satisfaction. Among the most compelling revelations uncovered is the positive sentiment that defines tourists' perceptions of Lesotho. Whether captivated by the awe-inspiring landscapes or touched by the genuine warmth and hospitality of the Basotho people, visitors consistently expressed admiration and delight in their encounters with destination Lesotho. We really hope that you will find this report both helpful and insightful. Together, let us chart a course toward a vibrant and sustainable tourism industry where the spirit of quality service, adventure and discovery knows no bounds. Building better tourism 31. Introduction Destination Lesotho Located in the southern part of Africa, Lesotho is renowned for its breath-taking landscapes, including mountains, nature reserves, rivers, waterfalls, hot springs, diverse flora and fauna, cave formations, bird species as well as picturesque villages. These features make it possible for Lesotho to offer a wide range of activities and attract a variety of people from different parts of the world including adventure enthusiasts, sportsmen, sightseers, academics and culture lovers. 2. Background Tourism stands as a cornerstone of Lesotho's economic landscape and central to this narrative is the image Lesotho projects onto the international fraternity. As Cai (2002) rightly articulates this, a destination's image encapsulates the perceptions held in the collective memory of tourists, shaping their decisions and interactions and making it important on how a destination is viewed. Recognizing this imperative, both the government and the Lesotho Tourism Development Corporation (LTDC) have embarked on initiatives to comprehend tourists' perceptions of Lesotho and this task is aimed at reshaping and reinforcing the image of Lesotho, so that it enhances its appeal to both domestic and regional tourists. As underscored by Kotler et al. (2010), enhancing the quality of tourist attractions as well as supporting products and services is instrumental in elevating tourist satisfaction and fostering emotional connections with a destination's offerings. Such connections, as noted by Truong & King (2009), engender loyalty among tourists, paving the way for return visits and positive advocacy to potential tourists. Despite the pivotal role of perceptions in shaping tourism outcomes, Lesotho has seen limited studies into tourists' perceptions and due to this gap, this study seeks delve deeper into tourists' perceptions of destination Lesotho and inform strategic decisions aimed at fostering sustainable tourism development. Through an examination of tourists' motivations for selecting destination Lesotho, their overall satisfaction with the destination, their profiles and travel behaviour, this report will unearth actionable insights that will inform strategic decisions for the improvement of Lesotho's position in the global tourism landscape. 43. Objective As a supplement to the routine collection of tourist arrivals data from all ports of entry on a daily basis, it has become imperative to gather additional data in order to enrich the understanding of visitors' perceptions and travel patterns to Lesotho. The specific objectives of the study are as follows: i. To identify the motivations for selecting Lesotho as a preferred destination. ii. To establish profiles, travel patterns and behaviours of visitors to Lesotho. iii. To ascertain the opinions and level of satisfaction of tourists regarding the tourism services and facilities in Lesotho. 4. Methodology Data Collection Data was collected through the use of visitor logbooks at Maseru Bridge and Pioneer Information Offices. The logbook covered a range of variables including country of residence, sources of information about Lesotho, purpose of visit, length of stay and general opinions about destination Lesotho. Data was collected from January 2019 to December 2023. Sampling Technique Convenience sampling technique was employed for the study whereat visitors who made inquiries at Maseru Bridge and Pioneer information offices about certain places and tourism products as well as those who have travelled in the country were encouraged to fill in the logbooks to share their views, experiences and perception about Lesotho. A total of 4172 visitors filled in the books and all the records were included for analysis. Data Analysis Descriptive analysis was used to highlight the basic characteristics of the data while content analysis was employed to refine the key points from the qualitative data gathered. 55.
